speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back to Git Going with GitHub. This is episode 25: Releases, Tags, and Insights. I am Alex, and today we are turning Releases, Tags, and Insights from a list of instructions into a working mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will stop us whenever the instructions sound simple on paper but might feel different with a keyboard and screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Semantic versioning, GitHub Releases, and repository analytics. That is the surface description. Underneath it, we are building judgment: where to focus, what to ignore, and how to verify the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are not using the audio as a shortcut around learning. We are using it to make the learning easier to enter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A good audio lesson gives someone enough context to try the work with confidence, even before they open the written material.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Understanding Versioned Releases and Repository Activity: Two things help you understand a repository's health and your place in it: releases (the versioned snapshots your contributions land in) and insights (the activity metrics that show how a project grows).

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Releases and Tags has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: What Is a Release? Then: Releases vs. Tags vs. Branches. Next: Navigating Releases on GitHub. Last: Understanding Version Numbers. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Reading Release Notes; For Maintainers: Creating a Release; Draft and Pre-Release States; and Accessibility Agents: /draft-release. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Repository Insights has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: What Is the Insights Tab?; Navigating to Insights; Pulse - Recent Activity Summary; and Contributors - Who Builds the Project. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Traffic - Who Visits the Repo; Commits and Code Frequency; Dependency Graph; and Network and Forks. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on 1. What Is a Release? A release is a named snapshot of a repository at a specific point in history, packaged and published for users. This is the part to say slowly: For software projects, a release typically includes.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. A version number (e.g., v2.1.0). Release notes explaining what changed. Links to download the compiled or packaged software (if applicable). Source code archives (automatically generated by GitHub).

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: these three concepts are closely related.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. The relationship has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Every release is backed by a tag. A tag without a release is just a version marker in the git history. GitHub automatically creates a tag when you create a release.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Finding the Releases Page has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Look on the right sidebar for a "Releases" section showing the latest release. Then: Activate "Releases" or the version link to go to the full releases page. Next: Alternatively: navigate to github.com/owner/repo/releases. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put The Releases List Page into plain language. Each release appears as a section.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. The release title (usually the version number). A badge: "Latest" (most recent stable), "Pre-release", or nothing (older release). Publication date. The tag name. Release notes (the main body). Asset downloads (if included).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Navigating Releases has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. On the repository home page, press H or 3 to find the "Releases" heading in the right sidebar, then K to the version link -- Enter opens the latest release directly. On the Releases list page, each release is a heading -- use 2 or 3 to jump between releases and arrow down to read the notes inline. The "Latest" badge appears next to the most recent stable release -- listen for it to distinguish stable releases from pre-releases or older versions. The Releases link on the repo home page sits in the right sidebar -- at high zoom the sidebar may collapse below the main content; scroll down to find it. Release notes use Markdown headings (Breaking Changes, New Features, Bug Fixes) -- zoom in and scan the bold section labels for quick triage. The green "Latest" badge next to the newest stable release is small -- zoom to 150%+ to spot it among the release list entries.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because most projects follow Semantic Versioning (semver): MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Examples: Pre-release suffixes (not part of semver core, but common).

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. v2.0.0 → major release, breaking changes possible. v2.1.0 → new features added. v2.1.3 → three bug fixes since v2.1.0. v2.1.0-alpha.1 → early preview, may be unstable. v2.1.0-beta.2 → feature-complete but still testing. v2.1.0-rc.1 → release candidate, nearly final.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. Release notes document what changed. Put another way, high-quality release notes categorize changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like What's New in v2.1.0; Breaking Changes; - The preferences.md format has changed - see the migration guide; New Features; - Added /project-status command ( 42, @alice); - Added /onboard-repo command ( 38, @bob); Bug Fixes; - Fixed keyboard navigation in the. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 6. For Maintainers: Creating a Release: this section is relevant if you become a maintainer or contribute to a project that asks you to prepare releases.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating a Release from the GitHub UI. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating a Release from the GitHub UI has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Navigate to github.com/owner/repo/releases/new; Choose a tag: Type a new version tag (e.g., v2.1.0) - GitHub will create it on publish, or select an existing tag if you already created one; Choose a target branch: The branch the tag will be applied to (usually main); and Release title: Usually the same as the tag (e.g., v2.1.0). That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Creating a Release from the GitHub UI, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is release notes: Write manually, or click "Generate release notes" - GitHub auto-generates from merged PR titles since the last release. Step two is assets: Optionally attach compiled binaries or other files. Step three is pre-release toggle: Mark as pre-release if it's alpha/beta. Step four is draft toggle: Save as draft to prepare without publishing immediately. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: the /draft-release command generates structured release notes automatically from your merged PRs - much faster and more consistent than writing them manually.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Learning Cards: Creating a Release has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. The release creation form at github.com/owner/repo/releases/new has several fields -- Tab through them in order: tag selector (combobox), target branch, title input, release notes textarea, asset upload, pre-release checkbox, draft checkbox, and publish button. The tag selector is a combobox -- type a new version tag (e.g., v2.1.0) and the option to create it appears; arrow down and press Enter to select. The "Generate release notes" button auto-fills the textarea with merged PR titles -- after clicking it, arrow through the generated notes to verify accuracy before publishing. The release creation page uses a single-column form layout -- zoom in and the form fields stack vertically for easy scanning. The "Pre-release" and "Draft" checkboxes are at the bottom of the form, below the release notes editor -- scroll past the potentially long textarea to find them. The "Generate release notes" button is a small link-style button near the release notes textarea -- look for it above or beside the text area at high magnification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 7. Draft and Pre-Release States. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 7. Draft and Pre-Release States is still useful because draft release: Saved but not published. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Only visible to repository collaborators.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put 8. Accessibility Agents: /draft-release into plain language. The /draft-release command automates release note generation from your merged pull requests. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What it produces. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What it produces has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Auto-categorized changes: Breaking Changes, Features, Bug Fixes, Dependencies. Each PR listed with number, title, and author. A full changelog link. Markdown formatted and ready to paste into GitHub's release editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because see also: The /release-prep command runs a complete pre-release checklist (milestone status, open PRs, CI health, security, and then generates release notes) - useful when preparing a release from scratch rather than just generating notes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like v2.1.0; New Features; - Add /project-status command ( 42, @alice); - Add /onboard-repo for first-time repo scanning ( 38, @bob); Bug Fixes; - Fix keyboard navigation in triage workflow ( 51, @charlie); - Correct screen reader announcement for merged PRs ( 48,. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 9. What Is the Insights Tab? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 9. What Is the Insights Tab?: The Insights tab shows quantitative activity data for a repository. The next useful detail is concrete: It is primarily read-only and chart-heavy - but all underlying data is also presented in tables that screen readers can navigate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Who can see Insights has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Anyone can see Insights on a public repository. Private repository Insights require collaborator access.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What Insights does NOT show. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What Insights does NOT show has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Individual user data in detail (e.g., lines per commit per person). Real-time data (most views update daily or weekly). Code quality scores.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 10. Navigating to Insights. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 10. Navigating to Insights has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Navigate to the repository's main tab bar; Find and select the Insights tab; and The Insights sub-navigation has multiple views listed on the left (or top on smaller screens). Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 11. Pulse - Recent Activity Summary. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: pulse is the Insights landing page. The listener should be able to check this: It summarizes activity in a chosen time period (last 24 hours, 7 days, or 30 days).

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Open issues / Closed issues - net change in the time period. Open pull requests / Merged pull requests / Closed pull requests - with counts. Authors: N contributors pushed M commits to N branches. A list of merged PRs (each linked to the PR).

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. What it tells you as a contributor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Is this project actively maintained? (Are PRs being merged regularly?). Is there a backlog? (Many open issues vs. few closures). Is the maintainer responsive? (Time between PR open and merge).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Pulse (Recent Activity Summary). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Pulse (Recent Activity Summary) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Pulse is the Insights landing page -- after entering the Insights tab, you land here; use 2 to jump between section headings (Merged PRs, Opened Issues, etc.). Each section lists linked PR or issue titles -- press 3 to jump between individual items, or K to navigate the links directly. Use the time period selector (last 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days) at the top to scope the summary -- Tab to it and select with arrow keys. Pulse displays activity counts in large numbers at the top of the page -- zoom in to read the open/closed/merged totals. The merged PR and opened issue lists below the summary use standard link formatting -- increase font size to scan titles comfortably. The time period toggle (24h, 7d, 30d) sits near the top -- it can be small at default zoom; look for the underlined active period indicator.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put 12. Contributors - Who Builds the Project into plain language. The Contributors view shows a bar chart of commits over time, with each contributor represented by a different color. The useful version is: Below the chart is a sortable table.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The table (accessible). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The table (accessible) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Username. Number of commits (with a link to filtered commit history). Additions (lines added). Deletions (lines removed). Sorted by total commits by default.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. Why this matters as a new contributor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. You can see how active top contributors are. After your first merged PR, your name appears here (with your commits). You can link to your section (github.com/owner/repo/graphs/contributors) as proof of contribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Screen reader. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Screen reader: The chart is a canvas graphic - not directly readable. The next useful detail is concrete: The table below it is fully accessible.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Learning Cards: Contributors has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. The contributor chart is a canvas graphic that screen readers cannot access -- skip it and navigate to the data table below with T (next table) which contains the same information in accessible form. The table has sortable column headers (Commits, Additions, Deletions) -- Tab to a header and press Enter to sort; the table reloads with the new sort order. Each contributor's username is a link to their filtered commit list -- activate it to see every commit that person made to this repository. The bar chart uses color to distinguish contributors -- the table below provides the same data as text; zoom in on the table if the chart colors are hard to differentiate. Sorting by Commits (default) puts top contributors first -- at high zoom, the table may require horizontal scrolling to see the Additions and Deletions columns. After your first merged PR, your username appears in this table -- link to it (github.com/owner/repo/graphs/contributors) as proof of contribution in your portfolio.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 13. Traffic - Who Visits the Repo. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 13. Traffic - Who Visits the Repo: traffic shows who is viewing and cloning the repository. That matters in practice: Available only to repository owners and collaborators with push access.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Why this matters has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. If the README is the most-viewed file, documentation improvements have high impact. If traffic spiked when a blog post linked the repo, that's a good signal for community growth.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 14. Commits and Code Frequency. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: commits shows commit frequency over the past year, by week. The listener should be able to check this: Code Frequency shows additions and deletions per week as an area chart.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Active development periods (many commits). Dormant periods (no commits) - a project with 6+ months of inactivity may be unmaintained. Release sprints (burst of commits before a release tag). Heavy refactoring.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Do not treat 15. Dependency Graph as decoration. Dependents - repositories that depend on this one - is the "used by" count you see on the right sidebar of popular packages.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. What your project depends on (libraries, packages). What depends on your project (if others import your repo).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Enabling/viewing. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Enabling/viewing is still useful because why it matters for security: The dependency graph feeds Dependabot. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: If a vulnerability is found in a library, Dependabot uses this graph to identify which repos use the affected version and opens automated PRs to update them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Settings → Security & Analysis → Dependency graph → Enable. Navigate to Insights → Dependency graph.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Put 16. Network and Forks into plain language. The Network view shows a graphical branch/fork network - who has forked the repo, what branches exist, and how they diverge. The useful version is: The network graph is a canvas visualization not accessible to screen readers. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 17. Community Standards. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The Community Standards view (found in the Insights sidebar or in the main repository homepage's "Recommended" sidebar section) shows a checklist of community health files. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: A fully green checklist signals a well-maintained project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Screen reader has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like H / 2 → "Community Standards" section heading; Tab → Each checklist item (links to add missing files). Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Comparing two releases (the Changelog). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Comparing two releases (the Changelog): The compare URL (/compare/v1.0.0.v2.0.0) shows a diff of all commits and merged PRs between two tags.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. Most Insights charts are canvas or SVG visuals. Put another way, they announce as "image" or "graphic" to screen readers. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 19. Accessibility Agents: /my-stats and /team-dashboard. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 19. Accessibility Agents: /my-stats and /team-dashboard: instead of navigating GitHub's chart-heavy Insights UI, Accessibility Agents provides two commands that deliver the same data in text form directly in VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on /my-stats. Shows your personal contribution statistics across all tracked repos.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. PRs opened and merged. Issues filed and closed. Reviews conducted. Commits pushed. Compare to previous period.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on /team-dashboard. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: shows team-wide activity across tracked repos - who contributed what, response times, review coverage. The listener should be able to check this: Next: Appendix T: Community and Social Back: Appendix R: Projects Deep Dive Teaching chapter: Chapter 08: Open Source Culture.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Per-person contribution summary. Review coverage (how many PRs got reviews). Open issues by assignee. CI health across team repos.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 25. Next in the series is episode 26, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
